title: Pommes Dauphinoise
subtitle: Gratin dauphinois — thin potato slices baked in cream and garlic until golden
description: Pommes dauphinoise is the great French potato dish: wafer-thin potato slices, garlic, double cream, and nothing else, layered in a gratin dish and baked slowly until the cream reduces and the top is deeply golden and slightly set. The classical version contains no cheese — the cream alone sets the dish — though a little Gruyère on top is a common and welcome variation. It is the definitive accompaniment to roasted meats.
sourceNotes: Gratin dauphinois is one of the classic dishes of the Dauphiné region of southeast France. The earliest documented recipe appears in 1788 from a dinner given by the Duke of Clermont-Tonnerre. Escoffier's Le Guide Culinaire (1903) codifies the technique used here.

recipeTools.notes: A 30 x 22 cm gratin dish or similar shallow ovenproof dish — about 5 cm deep. The wide, shallow shape promotes even browning.
For slicing the potatoes to 3 mm — thin enough to cook through in the cream. A sharp knife and steady hand also work, but a mandoline is much faster.
For heating the cream with the garlic before pouring.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dish with a golden-brown crust formed by the upper surface being exposed to high heat. The crust in dauphinoise forms from the reduced cream and potato starch rather than cheese.

ingredients.notes: Charlotte or Desiree — waxy varieties hold their shape in cream. Maris Piper work but soften more. Peeled, sliced 3 mm thick.
2 crushed for the cream, 1 halved for rubbing the dish

steps: Heat the oven to 160 °C fan (180 °C / gas 4). Rub the gratin dish generously with the cut side of the halved garlic clove, then butter it well.
Warm the cream in a medium saucepan with the crushed garlic over a low heat until just below simmering. Do not boil. Season with salt, pepper, and a light grating of nutmeg. Remove the garlic.
Layer the potato slices in the dish, overlapping slightly, seasoning each layer lightly with salt. Pour the warm cream over the top — it should come just below the top layer of potatoes.
Press the surface down gently with your hand so the potatoes are submerged in cream as much as possible.
Bake for 75–80 minutes until the top is deep golden and a knife passes through the centre with no resistance. If the top is browning too fast after 45 minutes, cover loosely with foil.
Leave to rest for 10 minutes before serving — it sets slightly as it cools and portions more cleanly.

content.text: The classical recipe contains no cheese. The

crust forms from the cream and potato starch alone. Do not wash the potato slices after cutting — the starch on the surface is what thickens the cream into a cohesive set during baking. Rinsing produces a watery dish.

Heat the oven to 160 °C fan. Rub the gratin dish with the cut side of a halved garlic clove, then butter it. Warm {{double-cream}} of double cream in a small saucepan with 2 crushed garlic cloves until just below simmering. Season with salt, pepper, and a light grating of nutmeg. Remove the garlic.

Layer {{waxy-potatoes}} of thinly sliced potatoes in the dish, overlapping slightly, seasoning each layer lightly with salt. Pour the warm garlic cream over the top — it should nearly reach the uppermost layer. Press the surface down gently. Bake for 75–80 minutes until deep golden and a knife passes through the centre without resistance. If the top browns too fast, cover loosely with foil after 45 minutes. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.
